verses.text: 'If thou wilt return... return unto me'; 'break up your fallow ground' and 'circumcise yourselves to the LORD.' True return is inward. God seeks not mere reform but a heart broken up and given to Him.

'If thou wilt return... return unto me'; 'break up your fallow ground' and 'circumcise yourselves to the LORD.' True return is inward. God seeks not mere reform but a heart broken up and given to Him.

The trumpet sounds alarm; 'the destroyer of the Gentiles is on his way,' a lion from his thicket. Invasion looms. The warnings of God's prophets call a careless people to flee to repentance.

In that day the king and priests shall be dismayed; a scorching wind, not for winnowing, comes against the people. Judgment is fierce. The blast of God's anger is no gentle breeze but a destroying storm.

The foe comes up 'as clouds,' his chariots like a whirlwind; 'O Jerusalem, wash thine heart from wickedness, that thou mayest be saved.' Cleansing is urged. Even at the brink, repentance can yet bring deliverance.

Watchers come from a far country against Judah's cities, for 'thy way and thy doings have procured these things.' Sin invites the siege. The calamity is the harvest of the nation's own rebellion.

Jeremiah cries, 'My bowels... I am pained at my very heart,' for his people are foolish, 'wise to do evil, but to do good they have no knowledge.' His grief is deep. The prophet weeps over a people clever only in sin.

He sees the earth 'without form, and void,' the heavens dark, the cities broken, as if creation were undone. Judgment looks like a return to chaos. So terrible is sin's harvest that the prophet pictures the world unmade.

The land shall be desolate, 'yet will I not make a full end'; the doomed city, decking herself in vain, finds her lovers despise her. Mercy limits the ruin. God judges severely but preserves a remnant for Himself.
